topic: The Himalayan System — Ranges, Passes & Peaks

questions.text: Which is the highest mountain peak entirely within India (not on a border)?
options: Kanchenjunga (8,586 m)
Nanda Devi (7,816 m)
Kamet (7,756 m)
Everest (8,849 m)
questions.explanation: Nanda Devi (7,816 m) in Uttarakhand's Chamoli district is the highest peak entirely within India — its summit lies completely on Indian territory. Kanchenjunga (8,586 m) is higher and is commonly called 'India's highest peak' because it sits on the India-Nepal border in Sikkim — but since it is a border peak, not entirely within India, the answer depends on how the question is phrased. When SSC asks 'highest peak entirely within India,' the answer is Nanda Devi. Everest (8,849 m) is on the Nepal-China border — not in India at all. Kamet (7,756 m), also in Uttarakhand, is the second highest peak entirely within India. Nanda Devi is surrounded by the Nanda Devi Biosphere Reserve and was the site of a controversial 1965 CIA nuclear device operation.
questions.source: SSC CGL — 'highest peak entirely within India' (Nanda Devi) vs. 'highest peak in India' (Kanchenjunga, border peak) is one of the most tested distinctions in Indian geography

questions.text: Nathu La pass is located in which state and connects India to which country?
options: Arunachal Pradesh — connects India to Myanmar
Uttarakhand — connects India to Nepal
Sikkim — connects India to China (Tibet)
Himachal Pradesh — connects India to China (Xinjiang)
questions.explanation: Nathu La pass (altitude ~4,310 m) is located in Sikkim and connects India to China's Tibet Autonomous Region through the Chumbi Valley. It was a key part of the ancient Silk Route trade between India and Tibet/China. Nathu La was closed after the 1962 Sino-Indian War and reopened for bilateral trade in June 2006 — making it a significant diplomatic event in India-China relations. It is also used by Indian pilgrims traveling to Kailash Mansarovar via the Sikkim route. Jelep La is another pass in Sikkim connecting to Tibet, but less prominent than Nathu La. Bomdi La is in Arunachal Pradesh (not connecting to Myanmar — that's Diphu Pass). Shipki La in HP connects to Tibet (Xinjiang border is farther north, via Karakoram Pass in Ladakh).
questions.source: SSC CGL — Nathu La (Sikkim, India-China/Tibet) is a frequently tested pass identification question; its reopening in 2006 and Silk Route connection are additional tested details

questions.text: The Atal Tunnel (Rohtang Tunnel) was opened in 2020. Which pass does it bypass, and which two regions does it connect?
options: Bypasses Zoji La pass; connects Srinagar to Kargil
Bypasses Rohtang Pass; connects Manali (Kullu Valley) to Lahaul-Spiti valley in Himachal Pradesh
Bypasses Banihal Pass; connects Jammu to the Kashmir Valley
Bypasses Shipki La; connects Kinnaur to Spiti valley
questions.explanation: The Atal Tunnel (officially named Atal Rohtang Tunnel), inaugurated on 3 October 2020 by PM Modi, runs for 9.02 km through the Rohtang Pass area in Himachal Pradesh. It connects Manali (in the Kullu Valley) to Sissu in the Lahaul-Spiti valley, at an altitude of approximately 3,100 m (the tunnel goes under the pass, which is at 3,978 m). Before the tunnel, Lahaul-Spiti was cut off from the rest of HP for about 6 months every year when Rohtang Pass was snow-blocked. The tunnel provides all-weather connectivity. The Jawahar Tunnel (1956) does the same for Banihal Pass in J&K, connecting Jammu to Srinagar. The Z-Morh Tunnel connects Sonmarg to the Zoji La approach in J&K.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Atal Tunnel / Rohtang Tunnel (opened 2020, Rohtang Pass, Manali to Lahaul-Spiti) is a current affairs-meets-geography question; opened in 2020 makes it recent and frequently tested

questions.text: The Siachen Glacier is the world's highest battlefield. In which mountain range is it located?
options: In the Greater Himalayas (Himadri) of Uttarakhand
In the Karakoram Range of Ladakh
In the Zaskar Range on the Ladakh-Tibet border
In the Lesser Himalayas (Himachal range) of Himachal Pradesh
questions.explanation: The Siachen Glacier is located in the Karakoram Range, in the Ladakh region. It is approximately 70-76 km long (the longest glacier in India) and is situated at altitudes of 5,400-7,000 m. India controls the Siachen Glacier and the surrounding area (the Saltoro Ridge); Pakistan disputes this control. It is called the world's highest battlefield because both Indian and Pakistani military posts are maintained at extreme altitudes — some posts above 6,000 m. The glacier is not in the Greater Himalayas (Himadri) which are the main Himalayan chain; the Karakoram is a separate, trans-Himalayan range lying north and northwest of the main Himalayas. The Zemu Glacier in Sikkim is the largest glacier east of the Karakoram in India (associated with Kanchenjunga).
questions.source: SSC CGL — Siachen Glacier (Karakoram Range, Ladakh, largest glacier in India, world's highest battlefield) is a standard multi-fact identification question

questions.text: Kanchenjunga is described as 'the highest peak in India.' Why is this statement accurate but also potentially misleading?
options: It is accurate because Kanchenjunga is on Indian soil in Sikkim; it is misleading because a higher peak — Everest — is often mistakenly claimed by India

It is accurate because Kanchenjunga (8,586 m) sits on the India-Nepal border (Sikkim/Nepal), making it the highest point where India has sovereignty; it is potentially misleading because its summit straddles the border (so it is equally Nepal's highest peak), and Nanda Devi (7,816 m) is higher than all peaks entirely within India — making it 'highest entirely in India'
It is accurate because Kanchenjunga is the world's highest peak; it is misleading because students confuse it with Everest
It is accurate only in the sense that Kanchenjunga is visible from India (Darjeeling); it is actually located entirely in Nepal
questions.explanation: The 'highest peak in India' question has two valid answers depending on the exact phrasing: (1) 'Highest peak in India' including border peaks = Kanchenjunga (8,586 m) on the Sikkim-Nepal border, 3rd highest in the world. (2) 'Highest peak entirely within India' = Nanda Devi (7,816 m) in Uttarakhand. Kanchenjunga's summit lies on the international border, so it is simultaneously India's highest and Nepal's highest — a conceptual oddity. For SSC, 'highest peak in India' almost always expects Kanchenjunga; 'highest peak entirely within India' expects Nanda Devi. Everest (8,849 m, world's highest) is NOT in India — it is on the Nepal-China border. Never say Everest is India's highest peak in any exam context.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the Kanchenjunga (border peak, India's highest) vs. Nanda Devi (entirely in India) distinction is the single most tested conceptual trap in Indian peaks questions

questions.text: What is the significance of Shipki La pass, and which river enters India through it?
options: Shipki La is in Uttarakhand; the Ganga enters India through it from Tibet
Shipki La is in Himachal Pradesh (Kinnaur district) on the India-China border; the Sutlej River enters India through this pass from Tibet
Shipki La is in Sikkim; the Teesta River enters India through it from Bhutan
Shipki La is in J&K; the Indus River enters India through this pass from Tibet
questions.explanation: Shipki La (altitude ~3,933 m) is located in Kinnaur district of Himachal Pradesh, on the India-China border. The Sutlej River — one of the major tributaries of the Indus system — enters India through this pass from Tibet (where it originates near Lake Rakshastal, close to the source of the Indus and Brahmaputra). The Sutlej then flows through Himachal Pradesh and Punjab before entering Pakistan. Shipki La has historically been a trade route. The Indus River enters India through a different pass — the Indus enters Ladakh (J&K) from Tibet near Demchok. The Brahmaputra (called Tsangpo in Tibet) enters India through a great gorge in Arunachal Pradesh, not through a named pass. Each major river's entry point from Tibet is testable: Indus → Ladakh; Sutlej → Shipki La (HP); Brahmaputra → Arunachal Pradesh gorge.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because 'which river enters India through Shipki La' is a specific rivers-passes crossover question; the Sutlej (not Indus, not Ganga) is the answer and requires knowing both passes AND river geography simultaneously

questions.text: The Purvanchal hills are described as the 'eastern extension of the Himalayas.' What geological evidence supports this, and how are they different from the main Himalayas?
options: Purvanchal hills are not actually related to the Himalayas — they are a separate range formed by African tectonic plate movement; they are different because they run north-south while the Himalayas run east-west

Purvanchal hills (Patkai, Naga, Manipur, Mizo Hills) share the same origin as the Himalayas — they formed when the Indo-Australian plate collided with the Eurasian plate, and the orogenic forces curved southward into northeastern India; however, they are lower (mostly below 3,000 m), more broken, covered by dense tropical jungle, and form the boundary with Myanmar rather than with China or Tibet

Purvanchal hills were formed by a separate collision between the Myanmar plate and the Indian plate — entirely different from Himalayan formation; they share no geological connection with the Himalayas
Purvanchal hills are ancient, stable ranges like the Peninsular Plateau, not young fold mountains; the 'Himalayan extension' label is a geographic metaphor, not a geological statement
questions.explanation: The Purvanchal hills are the curved eastern extremity of the Himalayan orogenic belt — formed by the same tectonic collision (Indo-Australian plate + Eurasian plate) but at the eastern termination of the main arc. Where the Himalayas run broadly northwest to southeast, they curve sharply southward at the eastern end (around the Namcha Barwa syntaxis in Arunachal Pradesh) and become the Purvanchal hills. Key differences from the main Himalayas: (1) Height — mostly 1,000-2,500 m, much lower than the main Himalayas; (2) Orientation — they run roughly north-south (parallel to the coast of Myanmar), unlike the main east-west Himalayan arc; (3) Vegetation — dense tropical and subtropical jungle (not alpine snowfields); (4) Strategic boundary — they separate India's northeast from Myanmar rather than from China/Tibet. The ranges in order (north to south): Patkai Hills → Naga Hills → Manipur Hills (Barail Range) → Mizo Hills (Lushai Hills). These hills, along with Bangladesh, form the geographic enclosure of India's northeast.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Purvanchal is listed in textbooks but rarely explained in terms of WHY it is a Himalayan extension or how it differs; the north-south orientation and lower elevation differences are the analytical content tested in harder papers

questions.text: What are 'Duns' in the context of Himalayan geography, and what is the most famous Dun in India?
options: Duns are the deep glacial lakes found in the Greater Himalayas (Himadri); the most famous is Dal Lake in Kashmir

Duns are flat, fertile valley basins formed between the Shiwaliks (Outer Himalayas) and the Lesser Himalayas (Himachal range); the most famous is Dehradun in Uttarakhand, which gives the city its name
Duns are high-altitude passes in the Greater Himalayas used by nomadic herders; the most famous is Rohtang Dun in Himachal Pradesh
Duns are the alluvial fans deposited by Himalayan rivers at the base of the mountains; the most famous is the Haridwar Dun near the Ganga exit point
questions.explanation: Duns (also spelled 'Doons') are flat-floored longitudinal valleys or structural basins formed in the zone between the Shiwaliks (Outer Himalayas) and the Lesser Himalayas (Himachal range). They are filled with alluvium deposited by rivers flowing within these valleys. The most famous Dun is Dehradun — which gives the city of Dehradun (capital of Uttarakhand) its name (Dehra = a temporary dwelling/camp; Dun = the valley type). Other Duns include Patlidun, Kotlidun, and Udhampur Dun (in J&K). Duns are structurally formed — they are synclines (downfolded portions) of the Himalayan structure, not river valleys in the conventional sense, though rivers do flow through them. They are generally fertile and have historically been important settlement zones. The distinction between a Dun (Himalayan foothill valley between Shiwaliks and Lesser Himalayas) and a valley like Kashmir (which is within the Lesser Himalayas) is worth noting.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because 'Dun' as a geographic term is rarely explained beyond 'Dehradun is in a Dun'; the structural origin (between Shiwaliks and Himachal range) and other examples (Patlidun, Kotlidun) are the testable content

questions.text: Why do Himalayan rivers carve such deep gorges in the mountain zones, and which is the deepest gorge in India?
options: Himalayan rivers carve deep gorges because they are young rivers with very steep gradients; the deepest gorge is the Indus Gorge near Attock in Punjab

Himalayan rivers are 'antecedent rivers' — they existed before the Himalayas rose; as the mountains uplifted, the rivers maintained their course by cutting downward through the rising rock; the deepest gorge in India is the Brahmaputra Gorge (near Namcha Barwa in Arunachal Pradesh), which is one of the deepest river gorges in the world
Himalayan rivers carve gorges because they are fed by monsoon rainfall alone, which creates seasonal flash floods that erode rock; the deepest gorge is the Chenab Gorge in J&K
Himalayan rivers carve gorges because they carry glacial meltwater with abrasive rock particles; the deepest gorge is the Teesta Gorge in Sikkim
questions.explanation: Himalayan rivers are technically 'antecedent rivers' — they originated before the Himalayas rose (they flowed through the Tethys Sea sediments). As the tectonic collision began pushing the land upward (around 50 million years ago), these rivers maintained their ancient courses by cutting downward through the rising rock — a process called 'antecedence.' This is why the Indus, Sutlej, and Brahmaputra flow in deep gorges THROUGH the mountain ranges rather than around them. The Brahmaputra Gorge (near Namcha Barwa syntaxis in Arunachal Pradesh, where the Brahmaputra makes its great bend from flowing west in Tibet to south into India) is one of the deepest gorges in the world — approximately 5,500 metres deep between Namcha Barwa (7,782 m) and the river floor. The Indus Gorge near Gilgit in Pakistan is another famous deep gorge. The antecedent river concept explains why rivers 'cut through' mountain ranges rather than being deflected by them.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the antecedent river concept is mentioned in textbooks but rarely explained; the Brahmaputra Gorge and the reason Himalayan rivers flow through (not around) mountain ranges is the analytical content tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: The Lipulekh Pass in Uttarakhand is associated with both a pilgrimage route and a territorial dispute. What are these two connections?
options: It is the entry point for Char Dham pilgrimage and is disputed between India and Pakistan

It is used by pilgrims going to Kailash Mansarovar (Tibet) via the Uttarakhand route, and India opened a road through it in 2020 — which Nepal immediately protested, claiming Lipulekh and the surrounding Kalapani-Limpiyadhura area is Nepali territory
It is the starting point of the Amarnath pilgrimage route and is disputed between India and China
It connects India to Nepal for Hindu pilgrimage to Pashupatinath, and is disputed between India and China over an infrastructure project
questions.explanation: Lipulekh Pass (5,334 m) in Pithoragarh district, Uttarakhand, is significant for two reasons: (1) Pilgrimage: It is one of the routes used by Indian pilgrims to reach Kailash Mansarovar in Tibet (China) — a sacred site for Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains. India, China, and Nepal all have routes to Kailash; the Lipulekh route through Uttarakhand is the most direct for Indian pilgrims. (2) Territorial Dispute: In May 2020, India inaugurated a road connecting Dharchula to Lipulekh Pass (the Lipulekh road, part of a strategic border roads project). Nepal immediately protested, asserting that the road passes through Nepali territory in the Kalapani-Limpiyadhura-Lipulekh area — a region Nepal claims based on the interpretation of the 1816 Sugauli Treaty. Nepal even passed a new political map through its Parliament in 2020 showing this region as Nepali. India rejected Nepal's claim. This is an ongoing diplomatic dispute. The broader region (including Kalapani) has been under Indian administrative control since 1962 but Nepal has not accepted this.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Lipulekh appears mainly as a pass name in lists; its dual significance (Kailash Mansarovar route AND the 2020 India-Nepal territorial dispute) connects geography with current affairs and is tested in harder papers

questions.text:
Match the mountain pass with its location (state/region) and what it connects:
1. Zoji La         A. Sikkim — India to Tibet (China); reopened for trade 2006
2. Rohtang Pass    B. J&K — Srinagar to Ladakh (Leh); on NH-1
3. Nathu La        C. HP — Manali (Kullu) to Lahaul-Spiti; bypassed by Atal Tunnel
4. Bomdi La        D. Arunachal Pradesh — strategic pass; 1962 Sino-Indian War
questions.explanation: Correct matches: (1) Zoji La → B. J&K — connects Srinagar to Kargil and Leh via National Highway 1; altitude ~3,528 m; snow-blocked in winter; critical supply route for Ladakh; Z-Morh tunnel under construction nearby. (2) Rohtang Pass → C. HP — connects Manali in Kullu Valley to Lahaul-Spiti; altitude ~3,978 m; snow-blocked ~6 months/year; Atal Tunnel (opened October 2020, 9.02 km) now provides year-round connectivity. (3) Nathu La → A. Sikkim — connects India to Tibet (China) via Chumbi Valley; altitude ~4,310 m; historic Silk Route; closed after 1962 war, reopened for trade in 2006. (4) Bomdi La → D. Arunachal Pradesh — strategic pass associated with the 1962 Sino-Indian War; China captured Tawang through passes in this area; altitude ~2,217 m. These four passes are the most frequently tested in SSC geography questions about Himalayan passes.
questions.source: Pass-to-location-connection matching (Zoji La/J&K/Srinagar-Leh, Rohtang/HP/Manali-Lahaul, Nathu La/Sikkim/China-trade, Bomdi La/Arunachal/1962) is the most frequently tested format for Himalayan passes in SSC CGL

questions.text: Which of the following statements about the Himalayan ranges is INCORRECT?
options: The Himadri (Greater Himalayas) is the innermost range, with average elevation ~6,000 m, and contains peaks above 8,000 m
The Shiwaliks (Outer Himalayas) are the youngest and outermost range, composed of unconsolidated sediment, with 'Duns' (flat valleys) formed between them and the Lesser Himalayas
The Lesser Himalayas (Himachal range) contain famous hill stations like Shimla, Mussoorie, and Darjeeling, and the Kashmir Valley is a structural basin within this zone
The Shiwaliks are the highest of the three ranges because they are the outermost and receive the most rainfall from the Bay of Bengal branch of the monsoon
questions.explanation: The INCORRECT statement is option D. The Shiwaliks are the LOWEST of the three main Himalayan ranges (600–1,500 m average elevation), NOT the highest. They are the outermost (southernmost) range. The sequence from north (inner) to south (outer) in terms of decreasing height: Himadri (~6,000 m avg, highest) → Himachal/Lesser Himalayas (3,700–4,500 m avg) → Shiwaliks (600–1,500 m avg, lowest). The Shiwaliks being outermost does mean they receive rainfall from the monsoon first, but their low elevation is not because of altitude of rainfall — it is because they are formed from unconsolidated, younger sediment that hasn't been uplifted as much. Options A, B, and C are all correct: Himadri characteristics (A); Shiwaliks with Duns (B); Lesser Himalayas with hill stations and Kashmir Valley (C).
questions.source: INCORRECT statement format for Himalayan ranges (Shiwaliks are LOWEST not highest) is a standard SSC trap; students confuse 'outermost' with 'highest' because they think outer = closer to rainfall

questions.text:
Arrange these Himalayan peaks in descending order of height (tallest first):
(i) Nanda Devi (ii) Kanchenjunga (iii) Everest (iv) K2 (v) Kamet
questions.explanation: Correct descending order: (iii) Everest = 8,849 m (world's highest; Nepal-China border) → (iv) K2 = 8,611 m (2nd highest in world; Karakoram, Gilgit-Baltistan/Pakistan-administered) → (ii) Kanchenjunga = 8,586 m (3rd highest in world; India-Nepal border, Sikkim; India's highest peak) → (i) Nanda Devi = 7,816 m (highest peak entirely within India; Uttarakhand) → (v) Kamet = 7,756 m (2nd highest entirely within India; Uttarakhand). The K2 vs. Kanchenjunga ordering is the key test point: K2 (8,611 m) is slightly higher than Kanchenjunga (8,586 m) — a 25-metre difference that is specifically tested. K2 is in Pakistan-administered territory; Kanchenjunga is on the India-Nepal border.
questions.source: Arranging peaks by height (Everest > K2 > Kanchenjunga > Nanda Devi > Kamet) is frequently tested; the K2 vs. Kanchenjunga height comparison (K2 slightly higher) and their locations are the specific traps
